posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
2016 Enfield Wine Co. Rosé of Pinot Noir Antle Vineyard - USA, California (6/14/2017)
Grill some salmon and pop a bottle of this for a perfect summer dinner. Opened while I was doing prep, the wine was linear and focused, with mostly ripe strawberry fruit and some back end expansiveness. Move forward to dinner and orange peel and honeysuckle blossom entered the picture. A little later it was too cold, and became mute, but time in the glass revived it to the fruit and floral state. Fresh and bright, it did not show grip, but glided across the palate. Easy and fun rose that worked like a charm with my meal.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Sounds tasty - and loved how you noted about this with and without food, and at different temperatures. Very helpful.

Cheers.

posts.name: bob parsons alberta
posts.cooked: I usually chill my reds down a little and watch the transformation as the wine opens and airs. Nice note David.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: FYI, as it got closer to room temp there were some pretty herbal and spice accents. I would not normally allow a rosé to get that warm.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
David,

I actually prefer to pour my Mourvedre Rose closer to room temperature. If it’s too cold, i find it dumbs down the aromas and the texture of the wine gets minimized. At least that’s what I find with mine.

If the rose is higher in acidity as most grenache-based and many pinot ones are, I prefer it more chilled.

Cheers.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Pretty sure this one is Pinot based.

posts.name: Andrew Demaree
posts.cooked: Super tasty wine, so easy to drink.

posts.name: John Lockwood
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

David,

I actually prefer to pour my Mourvedre Rose closer to room temperature. If it’s too cold, i find it dumbs down the aromas and the texture of the wine gets minimized. At least that’s what I find with mine.

If the rose is higher in acidity as most grenache-based and many pinot ones are, I prefer it more chilled.

Cheers.

Very good way to put this Larry. I would say the same about white wines with texture as well. The Enfield Rose is Pinot, but was foot tread whole cluster and underwent full ML - so it definitely has some texture. David - I don’t know if you saw my instagram post of the two Rose’ glasses side by side - but the wine physically turned redder after being open for a couple days while I was doing my tasting notes. Next bottle you pop, don’t be afraid to decant and server closer to “Red” temperature. Same as the “on skins” actually.

posts.name: Joe Chanley
posts.cooked: Call me naive but what is ‘back end expansiveness’?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just typed up my notes on this for a forthcoming Decanter article. I enjoyed it very much too.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: Thanks for the great tasting notes, David!
